content:
The common room was quiet, dimly lit by the warm glow of the hanging lanterns. Auri lounged on the couch, her legs and tail tucked beneath her, dark shorts and sheer black tights dotted with tiny stars. A lace-lined shirt peeked out from under an open button-up, the fabric relaxed over her shoulders as she shifted slightly. Twiggle rustled contentedly in her lap, leaves nudging against her hand as she absently ran her fingers across the foliage while reading a book titled *Shadows of Betrayal*. 

She took a sip from her tea. It was a black tea with a delightful mix of chocolate, cherry, and almond she’d found in one of the small shops on campus. While this particular blend was a little more expensive, there weren’t many things she wasted money on, or at least that’s how she justified the purchase. 

The warmth of the room, the quiet of the academy beyond their door, the soft crackle of the fire, and the gentle rustling of leaves made it easy to relax. She sank a little deeper into the cushions, eyes scanning the page with the kind of focus that made the outside world feel far away. 

<@155436835013656576>

Sol opened the door to the dorm and quietly slid into the common area. Three bags, respectively coated in gold, silver, and bronze colored magic, floated obediently behind him in an orderly line; they were stuffed to the brim with different colored fabrics, spools of threads, stencils, and what looked like a porcupine, but in reality was just an overcrowded pincushion that had been through the war that is designer Sol. A fourth bag bobbed lower than the rest; it was obvious that it was the heaviest of the lot, given its apparent attitude when asked to levitate itself rather than be carried.

Instead, Sol’s arms were occupied with the real prize: silky lengths of fabric that caught the light like water—soft, rich, and clearly expensive. One draped over his forearm in a pale, luminous, lavender shade; another gleamed darker, like it had stolen midnight from the night sky.

He paused when he saw Auri peacefully reading. Serenity seemed to have wrapped around her like a blanket: Twiggle nestled in her lap, her attention buried in the pages of her book, tea steaming faintly in her hand.

Sol didn’t move or say anything for a minute, not wanting to intrude and break the quiet moment. Then, with a warm smile, he greeted Auri.

“Hey Auri,” he said softly. His gaze flicked down to Twiggle, and his smile brightened a touch. “And hello to you too, Twig,” he added, tone affectionate and respectful, like he was greeting a tiny dignitary instead of a leaf-rustling companion.

He tilted his head towards his room down the hall, and the hovering bags drifted in that direction like a polite parade, treading carefully not to bump into anything important.

He re-adjusted the fabric in his arms as if it were more delicate than glass. “I’m just putting away supplies for something I’m working on, don’t mind me.”

That was all he offered for now. Usually, Sol loved to talk someone’s ear off about his special projects, but when he didn’t, it usually meant something else more important was running around in his head. 

Sol crossed into the kitchen area and carefully set the silky fabric down on the counter, smoothing it so it wouldn’t crease. It spilled slightly over the edge, catching the light in soft ripples.

Only after he was sure the material was still pristine did his eyes flick back to Auri’s hands. The book’s title caught his attention, and he pointed toward it with a slight tilt of his chin.

“Okay,” he said, voice low with interest, “what are you reading? *Shadows of Betrayal* sounds like the kind of book where absolutely nobody is okay by chapter three.”

Auri looked up briefly when she heard the door open, then shifted her gaze back to her book until Sol’s voice softly broke the silence. 

She looked to him, and when she saw it was Sol, her eyes softened and then widened slightly as she took in the procession of floating bags and the unmistakably luxurious fabric draped over his arms. 

Her tail gave a small, curious sway where it was tucked beneath her as she tucked a bookmark in her book, marking her place. “Hey Sol,” she said quietly, a smile blooming as her eyes traced the shimmer of lavender and midnight-dark cloth. 

Twiggle rustled at the sound of his name, leaves fluttering faintly, and Auri’s smile warmed as she glanced down and brushed her fingers over him. “He appreciates the respect,” she murmured softly, and her leafy companion’s branches swayed in pleased agreement. 

She watched Sol as he set the fabric down, noting the way he didn’t immediately explain what he was working on, which wasn’t like him. When he asked about her book, she lifted it slightly so he could see the cover, which featured an image divided by a sheer veil, the silhouette of a prince standing at its center. On one side stood a princess, her expression sharp and knowing; on the other, a maid, gentle and warm, her smile soft by comparison. 

“Right now,” Auri explained, “the prince just discovered that the princess he was in love with is engaged to someone else...” She set the book down on the arm of the couch. “A political opponent, nonetheless.” After she picked up her tea and took a sip, she added, “So you’re right— no one is okay.” 

“So,” she said gently, curiosity clear but not intrusive, “what are you working on? That looks important.”

Sol crossed into the living room, taking in the calm aura Auri crafted. He lowered himself onto the couch and angled his body toward her, one arm draped along the back. His eyes flicked to the book cover she’d shown him, and he let out a breath somewhere between a laugh and a sigh.

“Okay,” he murmured, warmth in his tone, “that *does* sound interesting.” His expression became a little more sympathetic, softening in the way it did when Sol wasn’t performing for attention. “And tragic,” he added.

The corner of his mouth lifted, and that typical Sol smile shone, warm like a lantern lighting up the dark.

“Still,” he said, voice gentle, “from my experience? Sad tales tend to have this habit of turning around when you least expect it.”

He looked at Auri, and he gave her a look that quietly implied: *especially the ones worth reading.*

When Auri asked what he was working on, Sol blinked, and his face flickered with a flash of surprise; it wasn’t her question, but moreso at himself. He had walked in with silk and a small storm of material trailing behind him like a royal procession, and he hadn’t said a word about it. His mouth parted like he was about to answer, and then he loudly laughed and rubbed the back of his neck with an awkward smile.

“Gods,” he said softly, shaking his head. “Sorry.” He looked at her with a sheepish grin, “A lot’s been going on in my head, usually I can’t shut up about my projects,” he laughed.

Sol snapped his fingers, and the air around him glittered. Shortly after, an overly stuffed red sketchbook shot out of his bedroom doorway like it had been launched from a cannon. Its pages fluttered, and stray ribbons and unsecured paper flopped out. The corners wobbled wildly, and for a split second, it looked like it might crash straight into Auri’s mug, but then it curved at the last second and dove toward Sol.

Almost immediately after the book left the room, excited stomping was heard in pursuit, and Bookie appeared in seconds, bounding after the sketchbook like an excited puppy chasing a stick. Bookie’s pages ruffled with every hop, and his eyes fluttered with excitement. The moment he reached Sol, he launched himself up with what could only be described as the grace of a newborn deer.

*THUMP*

Sol caught his sketchbook with one hand and Bookie with the other in a practiced motion, as if this were simply something that happened to him at least twice a week.

“Okay, okay, settle down,” Sol said fondly, rubbing Bookie’s spine with his fingertips until the enchanted book gave a pleased hum and calmed down.

He turned his attention back to Auri and flipped the sketchbook open, holding it out to her. On one page, roughly sketched clothing designs were alongside color swatches that matched the fabric he’d brought in. On the other page were more polished sketches: a tailored vest with subtle embroidery on the lapels; a silk shirt with a nice collar, though it was made of precious material, the shirt looked designed to be breathable and freely flowing; nice pants, and riding boots. Along the margins were notes in neat handwriting: measurements, preferences, and little reminders like *“don’t overdo the flare.”*

Sol continued petting Bookie as he watched for her reaction. Then he said: “Well, I figure since Caspian wants to get out there in the dating scene,” he began, his voice quietly pleased with himself, “I might as well have a nice outfit waiting for him for his first date.” He snorted, shaking his head as if the thought was hilarious and impossible at the same time. “Gods know when that’ll happen, though,” Sol added with a laugh. “But still, I believe preparedness is a virtue.”

He leaned back into the couch, sketchbook still open between them, and shot Auri a kind smile.

“I think he deserves to look and feel wanted, don’t you?”

Bookie let out a quiet mumble in his lap, as if he agreed.

Auri nodded when Sol described the book as ‘interesting yet tragic’, noting how his expression softened, and the way he lit up as he shifted the topic to something positive. “You’re right about that. It’s sad, yes,” she said, tracing the cover art with her finger. “But it’s also about realizing love is something that you earn. Something that shows up for you. When you like someone who doesn’t deserve it, it hurts, but when you find someone who does? That’s magic.” A wistful sigh escaped her as her gaze drifted far off for a moment, dreamy, before she focused again. “Anyway, I think when sad things happen, it’s so we can appreciate the good things even more, you know?”

She shrugged softly, tail curling in a relaxed loop beside her. “I’m only a quarter of the way in, but I have a feeling that the story isn’t really about the betrayal. It’s about learning what kind of love is actually worth keeping.” Her eyes lifted back to Sol. “They’re still friends at this point, but it’s clear that the maid,” she said, pointing to the woman on the cover, “and the prince are experiencing a bit of romantic tension, and unlike the princess, her inner thoughts reveal nothing but the intent to bring him happiness. No ulterior motives…just care”

The conversation shifted as Auri asked what Sol was working on, and after he confessed having a lot on his mind, he snapped his fingers, and the world around her sparkled. Auri watched the whole spectacle with open delight— the launched sketchbook, Bookie’s enthusiastic pursuit. Her brows lifted higher with each beat until a soft laugh escaped her. Twiggle rustled in her lap at the commotion, leaves fluttering in mild alarm before settling again as Auri smoothed a calming hand over him.

When he opened the sketchbook toward her, she leaned in without thinking, shifting on the couch so she could see properly. Her eyes traced the designs slowly, taking in the lines, the care in the tailoring, the neat notes in the margins. One hand hovered near the page, respectful, following the shapes without touching.“Oh,” she breathed, clearly impressed. “Sol…this is beautiful.” She smiled a little wider at the *Don’t overdo the flare* note on the page. 

At his explanation, her expression softened immediately. She looked up at him, warmth settling into her gaze. “I do think Caspian deserves that,” she said simply. “And I think this is a really lovely thing to do for him.” Her eyes dropped back to the sketches, thoughtful. “When that date finally happens, I fully expect a reveal. I want to see the finished look.” There was a brief pause before she carefully added,  “Sol…you said a lot’s been going on in your head. I find it hard to believe you are just worried about Caspian finding a date. What else is bothering you? You don't have to share it if you don't want to, but if you *do*, I'm here for you.”

Sol huffed out a fond laugh as Auri leaned in, watching as curiosity filled her eyes. He watched as her eyes traced the designs and how she held her hand above the page; Sol wouldn’t have minded if she touched the pages, but he appreciated her respectful restraint. He smiled when she complimented the designs; praise from Auri always made him feel good.

“Thanks,” he said with a warm voice. “I’m just hoping it doesn’t scream *Sol’s so invested in your love life and he couldn’t help but stay up past midnight to design you new clothes*.”

At Auri’s question, Sol’s smile dimmed a little, like someone turning down a light so it wouldn’t be too harsh. He glanced down at Bookie, who was shifting in his lap, then toward Twiggle. He tilted his head slightly, an apologetic expression taking root.

“Sorry for disrupting your relaxation with our talk, Twiggle; I didn’t mean to interrupt the whole relaxing ambiance you two had going.” 

He reached out, slowly and respectfully, and only if allowed, offered his fingers where he knew Twiggle liked being scratched. Then he leaned back into the couch and let out a heavy sigh. His hand resumed its steady, absent-minded stroking along Bookie’s spine, as the enchanted book melted into him.

“When you’re right, you’re right, Auri,” Sol said quietly.

He stared into space for a second, gathering his thoughts, before letting them spill out of his mouth like they’d been piling up all week.

“For one,” he started, a wry smile tugging at his mouth, “I actually have to *study*.”

He looked at Auri as if he were confessing a crime. “I know I act like I don’t care about grades,” he lightly shrugged, “but I do, a lot actually. I’ve been able to skate by on last-minute brilliance for a while now, but our last exams showed me it time to actually apply myself.”

He laughed before continuing.

“And then there’s my painting class,” he continued. “I don’t hate it, and I still love to paint, just,” he made a vague gesture with his free hand, “not like this, not as a requirement. It feels more like homework, and we both know I can’t stand doing homework.”

His fingers paused for a second on Bookie’s spine.

“I’m thinking I’d be happier in a fashion course instead. I want to learn about fashion history, design theory, how to improve my sketches; you know, things that will actually make my brain light up!” He smiled. “But that means joining a course mid-semester and doing a bunch of catching up, and I’m already behind in my other classes.”

His gaze dropped, and any humor in his voice left.

“Then there’s my dad,” he sighed. “He wrote to me a week after the Quasi exploded,” Sol said.“He genuinely sounded like he was worried about me.” He knit his brows slightly. “He asked if I was hurt or scared, and if I was eating and sleeping okay.” A short breath escaped him. “I don’t know if he said all those things because he realized I could’ve died or if he actually cares.” There was no venom in his words, just uncertainty. “And I think he wants to talk to me again soon,” he sighed.

“I want to believe he cares,” he admitted, “but I don’t know how to discern concern from control with him.”

Bookie let out a low, contented sound beneath his hand, something similar to a cat’s purr, and Sol softly smiled.

“Then there’s this little guy.”

He glanced down at Bookie, scratching his spine a little more deliberately, and his pages fluttered faintly.

“I kind of feel responsible for him now since the place we were hiding him isn’t an option anymore,” Sol said. “Usually I hide him in my bag, or in the hidden shelf in my room, but I don’t want him to spend his whole existence in hiding.”

He looked back at Auri, “So, I’ve been researching semi-permanent glamours, so he can roam around without being noticed.” His voice softened. “I want him to see things, be curious, experience everything living has to offer!”

Bookie’s purr deepened, and his pages shifted contentedly. Sol leaned back, quiet again, feeling like he just dumped years' worth of baggage between them.

“So yeah,” he finished, a small, sheepish smile returning. “It’s not just about Caspian’s first date.”

Auri shifted slightly on the couch as Sol spoke, angling her body toward him without crowding, one knee tucked beneath the other as if settling in for something important. When Sol apologized to Twiggle, her mouth curved into a soft, affectionate smile, and she glanced down at her leafy companion. “He's fine. He just likes being acknowledged,” she murmured, brushing her thumb along one of Twiggle’s leaves. The little plant rustled approvingly. 

Sol began to talk in earnest, and her expression changed as she focused on his words to make it clear she was listening to *all* of it, not just waiting for her turn to speak. Apparently, there was a lot on her friend's mind— studying, painting, his father, Bookie, and though he hadn’t mentioned him…Adrien. She paused for a moment, thinking about the best way to respond.

She decided to start with the first thing on Sol’s mind which was a pleasant surprise considering their first interaction began with her as his tutor.  “I’m proud of you, Sol,” she said, eyes lighting up with approval. “Let me know if you want to study with me again sometime. Not as a tutor and student— as *friends*”

“As for painting,” she went on head tilted thoughtfully, “I enjoy crochet, but I don’t plan to take any classes related to it. Not that any exist, but just because you love something doesn’t mean you love it *under pressure*. It’s okay for painting to be something that brings you joy, not something you need to study or be graded for. It makes sense, and it doesn’t sound reckless at all.” 

When he spoke about his father, her chest tightened just a little. Her gaze softened, eyes steady on his face. “I understand. It’s hard when you want to believe someone cares, but being afraid of what that care might cost you. I don’t think you need to decide what all of it means yet, but I do think you should talk to him, though you’re allowed to take that conversation at your own pace.”

Her attention dropped to Bookie as his pages fluttered. “And you’re right about Bookie,” she agreed. “No one should spend their whole life hidden away. Trust me, I would know.” A small, earnest smile appeared. “Semi-permanent glamours are tricky,” she added thoughtfully, slipping into that academic calm she carried so well. “But not impossible. If you want help researching or testing them safely, then I’m your girl— just say the word.” 

Once she’d addressed the concerns Sol had shared with her, she leaned her elbow against the arm of the couch and rested her cheek in her hand. “That’s a lot to be carrying, Sol,” she said. “I’m really glad you trusted me with it, and I hope some of what I said or offered to do will help.”

As Auri spoke, some of Sol’s stress began settle; her words helped click together the jumbled jigsaw puzzle of his mind. When she said she was proud of him, a warm smile beamed across his face. Her offer to study with him was the icing on the cake for everything, and he smiled even brighter.

“Yeah, I’d really like that,” he said. “I’ll definitely get more studying done if I do it around friends,” he laughed.

He released a breath of relief as they talked about his painting class and hobby.

“That’s exactly it,” Sol admitted. “I’ll always love painting, I just don’t love it when it’s being critiqued for a grade.” He glanced at his open sketchbook between them. “Fashion is a bit different; yes, it’s one of my favorite hobbies, but it’s something I want to make a career out of, so I want to learn everything I can about the industry before I graduate.” He tilted his head, mulling things over. “I guess I need to talk to my advisor about switching classes.” His mouth twisted slightly. “Which means a mountain of catch-up work,” he said, moaning into his palms. 

When Auri spoke about his father, Sol’s gaze drifted away, becoming contemplative. He wondered whether she’d say the same things if she knew the *whole* story between him and his dad. The years of emotional distance and heavy expectations that felt like scorching summer heat.

He didn’t say any of that aloud; instead, he slowly nodded.

“You’re right,” he said. “I don’t have to decide what it all means yet, I can just talk to him and figure it out afterwards.”

He smiled at Auri, though this time it was a little tinged with sadness; to the unobservant, it wouldn’t have been noticeable, but for a person with good discernment, it would’ve been clear as day. 

His blip of sadness disappeared when Bookie fluttered his pages. Sol's smile lit up again as he looked down at the enchanted book.

“See?” he said fondly. “You’ve got a whole support committee now!”

He glanced back up at Auri, eyes bright with relief.

“I’d love help from the resident student professor herself,” he said a little teasingly. “Glamour research, testing, safety checks, I want the whole curriculum!”

Bookie let out another pleased purr, rustling at the sound of her offer, clearly approving.

Then Sol’s attention slowly drifted back to Auri, and his expression shifted into something unmistakably cheeky; the corners of his eyes crinkled as a grin spread across his face.

“Hmm,” he hummed lightly, teasing already baked into his tone, “you want to know what’s going on between Adrien and me, *don’t you*?”

He laughed brightly before she could answer, leaning back into the couch, clearly enjoying this far too much.

“It’s written all over your aura,” Sol continued, gesturing vaguely around her body. “I can quite literally feel curiosity radiating off you.” He smiled playfully. “Go on,” he added, head tilting. “You can ask, I won’t bite.”

Auri *had* noticed the way Sol’s smile had faltered earlier. She’d heard the brief dip in his voice and the shadow that hadn’t quite left his eyes even after the laughter returned. The people who raised her hadn’t had facial expressions, and she’d grown fascinated by them, paying attention in ways most people took for granted. 

So instead of biting at the easy bait, she drew one knee up closer to herself on the couch and rested her forearm along it. Her tail gave a slow, thoughtful sway before settling. “Later,” she said gently, clearly in reference to Adrian, though his grin and playful tone caused her to crack a smile.“You’re right— I definitely want to know what’s going on between you two, but not at the expense of something that still feels…unfinished.”

“You don’t have to tell me everything,” she continued. “But you mentioned your father twice now, and both times your voice changed.” A small pause. “So…what’s your relationship with him actually like?” Then she smiled, softening the weight of the question just a touch, “I promise I’m not trying to dissect you emotionally,” she added lightly, “Tutoring occasionally comes with free psychoanalyzing.”

Sol tensed a bit when Auri pointed out his shift in tone; his shoulders tightened, and his breath hitched for a microsecond. He’d gotten very good at hiding his emotions when it pertained to his father, so he was surprised he apparently hadn’t gotten good enough to fool Auri.

He tilted his head and squinted at her with mock offense, pressing a hand dramatically to his chest.

“Really?!” Sol said, laughing. “It sure does feel like you’re holding a spotlight against my heart and poking me with a scalpel.” 

He gave an airy laugh, hoping his joke was enough to dodge the question, but it wasn’t, and his laughter faded into silence.

He leaned back into the couch, head tipping up until he was staring at the ceiling. The lantern light traced soft gold along his lashes and the bridge of his nose. His hand kept moving slowly along Bookie’s spine in an attempt to ground himself.

“Right now?” he said quietly. He paused, deep in thought. “The only way I could describe our relationship is strained.” His jaw tightened as he continued. “If you asked me a couple weeks ago, I honestly would’ve said I hated him.” The word landed heavier than he meant for it to. He exhaled after thinking some more. “But if you asked me when I was a kid?” A faint, breathy laugh slipped out. “I would tell you that he was my favorite person in the world,” he said with a bittersweet smile.

His gaze stayed fixed on the ceiling, but his expression softened as old memories flooded his mind.

“I saw him as this superhero designer when I was little,” Sol said. “I loved watching him sketch, cut, and pin stencils, and swear at fabric as it had personally betrayed him when they clashed.” A small smile tugged at his mouth. “Guess you know which parent I take after.”

He sighed and smiled gently.

“You know he and my mom argued for *days* trying to pick out my name after I was born.” Sol faintly shook his head. “Mom wanted to name me Corvus, but Father said he wanted to give me a name *‘destined for greatness.’*”

He scoffed quietly, a happy tone lying underneath.

“I don’t know how he pulled it off, but he convinced her on Sol’Régem. *King of the Sun*.”

His voice softened.

“He said I was always laughing up to the day my name was finalized, and that he saw my future always being bright because of it, so he pushed hard for the name.”

He paused again.

“He never used my full name growing up, though,” Sol said. “He thought it sounded too grown-up for a kid, and I’d have to grow into it.”

His smile came easier this time.

“So he called me his little *Sunbeam*.” The word lingered in the air, a hint of fragility and warmth accompanying it. 

Sol gently smiled and laughed again, much quieter than before.

“It’s funny,” he added, glancing toward Auri. “Adrien calls me that, too.”

His gaze drifted back to the ceiling, and he kept talking.

“He didn’t used to be so strict or cold; believe it or not, he was actually the fun parent.” Sol inhaled deeply, reflecting on his youth. “He was like the summer made corporeal.”

His fingers curled slightly against Bookie’s spine.

“He used to take me on work trips when he had to deliver designs to shops or buyers, and he always promised we’d do something fun after, like explore the city, get lunch, or see something really cool.” He looked over at Auri and smiled. “Sometimes he’d teach me magic,” he whispered.

Then the warmth in his voice thinned.

“But things changed when his businesses started closing.” A frown painted his face. “Suddenly Sunbeam became Sol, and after Lucien got sick, it was just Sol’Régem.” Silence filled the space for a moment. “He did call me Sunni in his most recent letter, though, so maybe we’re making progress,” Sol added quietly.

He didn’t know why his dad calling him his nickname mattered so much, but it just did. It felt like he was being transported back in time to simpler, happier days.

“After Lucien got put in stasis, it was like all of his summer warmth chilled into bitter winter wind,” 

Sol shut his eyes for a moment as he held Bookie closer to his body.

“He started trying hard to steer me away from fashion.” A humorless huff slipped out. “Politics, court ethics, strategy, persuasion; all the things he suddenly decided mattered more than passion.”

Sol’s face grew sad again.

“He even went as far as destroying most of my prototype designs and sketches.” He paused. “I think he thought if he erased the physicality of everything, it would erase the dream with it.”

He laughed bitterly.

“He’s even the one who enrolled me at Spark-Oath, and it wasn’t because he believed my magic was good enough to come here.” A faint, bitter smile crept onto his face. “He did it because he hoped I’d forget about art and get closer to court influence instead.”

Sol let out a short, humorless laugh.

“Which, obviously, backfired.” His gaze flicked briefly toward Auri. “I enrolled in every fine arts class I could the first chance I got, just to spite him.” He huffed again. “Which explains his reaction during Sponsorship Day.”

Silence stretched for a moment, and Sol exhaled, finally lowering his gaze from the ceiling, his eyes unfocused and misty.

“I don’t know if we’ll ever get back to Dad and Sunbeam,” he said softly. A small, tired smile curved his mouth. “But yeah, strained is the best way to describe us right now.”

Auri stayed where she was, quiet and still, giving Sol’s words the space they deserved. Her tail had stilled completely, curled close to her leg, and one hand rested absently against Twiggle as if grounding herself in the moment. When Sol finally looked away from the ceiling, she was already watching him with open attentiveness. 

Her brows knit slightly, not with pity, but empathy. “You had a father who saw you, delighted in you, and encouraged you. And then…that version of him slipped away along with your brother, replaced by expectations you never asked for.”

“It makes sense that you’re conflicted,” Auri continued. “That part of you still wants to believe in the man who called you Sunbeam, while the other part is protecting you from being hurt again.” She met his eyes fully now. “Those things don’t cancel each other out.”

Twiggle scurried to the side as Auri rose and stepped closer, slow enough to give Sol time to pull away if he wanted to. Then, if he allowed it, she wrapped her arms around him, holding him close.

“I’m so proud of you,” she said quietly. “For holding on to your passion when the world tried to erase it. You didn’t fail your father  by choosing passion, and you didn’t betray him for becoming yourself. He betrayed himself and you by turning his back.” Her gaze flicked to Bookie, then back to Sol as she leaned back slightly to look at him, her eyes shimmering with emotion.
“
“I don’t think progress has to mean going back to what things were,” she added. “Sometimes it just means finding a new way forward that doesn’t cost you who you are.” 

A small, reassuring smile touched her lips. “If he’s calling you Sunni again, maybe that’s a door cracked open, but you don’t have to rush through it. Until then…you have us.”

She stepped back slightly then, her hands sliding down the side of his arms to hold both of his hands if he let her. “You shared something of yours, so here’s something of mine: I’m very grateful to my mentor for being there when I didn’t have anyone else,” she admitted. “But I do wish I could have known my parents.” Her gaze drifted for just a moment. “Sometimes I wonder what they would have been like. If we would have had memories like that.”

Auri’s words stuck in places he didn’t usually let people touch, and he offered her a warm smile. When her arms wrapped around him, he returned the hug without hesitation, one arm wrapping around her back, the other carefully around Bookie so he didn’t jostle the enchanted book too much.

He briefly closed his eyes. “Thank you,” Sol whispered into her shoulder, “for all of that.” A warm look settled across his expression. “I’m really glad I have all of you guys, truly,” he smiled.

He pulled back enough to look Auri in the eyes, his smile small and his eyes a little shinier than usual.

At her admission about her mentor and about never knowing her parents, Sol’s expression gentled, and he squeezed her hands lightly.

“Sometimes,” he said thoughtfully, “it’s nice to imagine what things could’ve been like: the versions of people we never got to meet, the memories we never got to make.” He paused and tilted his head, that familiar hint of playful wisdom creeping in. “But you’ve got to be careful not to let the *‘what ifs’* grow too big.”

He gave Auri a crooked smile. “High expectations and fantasies have a bad habit of rampaging through your brain if you give them enough room.” He laughed softly. “Next thing you know, you’re in a full mental free-fall over a life that never actually existed if things don’t play out the way you expected.”

His thumb brushed gently over the back of her hand. “I think it’s okay to wonder, I think it’s beautiful to dream,” Sol said sagely, “just not at the cost of forgetting what’s *already* in front of you.” He smiled warmly at Auri as he looked into her eyes. “Just promise me while you dream you won’t forget about the people who *did* find you and the one who *chose* to raise and love you every day.”

Auri wrapped her arms around Sol’s waist, Bookie gently pressed between them and buried her face briefly in his chest, smiling and misty-eyed when they pulled back.

She didn’t pull her hands away from Sol when his thumb brushed over them. She didn’t rush to answer either. Instead, she let his words wash over her and settle in her heart. Her tail gave a slow, thoughtful sway behind her before curling loosely at her side. 

“I won’t,” she promised softly. “You’re right. I was found, and I was loved. I still am.” Her mouth curved faintly, and her fingers gave his hand a gentle squeeze in return. “And I don’t take the people who stayed lightly,” Auri continued. “You, Redd, Caspian, Vornakir…all of you chose me too, in your own ways.” 

She tilted her head slightly, a soft glimmer of fondness in her expression. “So I think I can manage a little wondering,” she said, a hint of playful confidence threading back in, “without losing sight of what I already have.” 

Her tail flicked once as she wiped her eyes with the back of her hand. “Okay, so…getting back on track.” Auri sniffed and huffed a small, shaky laugh. “Where were we? Finding a glamour spell for Bookie, right?” She gave Sol a steadier smile. “I can help you with that.”

Sol watched Auri as the steadiness returned to her voice and she wiped her eyes. Warmth settled in his chest, confirmation that this moment had been worth opening up for. When she mentioned all of the dormmates, he smiled, a quiet flicker of pride forming across his face. He squeezed her hand one more time before letting go, listening as Auri shifted the conversation back toward Bookie.

Sol laughed, seemingly having forgotten Bookie because of the emotional conversation. “Oh, right,” he said while snapping his fingers.

From down the hall, there was a faint sound of something thumping against the wall, followed by a soft *whoosh* of magic. A couple of seconds later, a thick textbook surrounded by golden magic drifted out of his room and sailed down the hallway. The book hovered for a second above Auri before gently dropping into her lap.

The cover read ***Illusory Theory: Hiding the Elephant in the Room***, and its pages were crammed with colorful sticky notes, some sticking out so far the book looked like it had grown feathers. Several folded notebook pages had also been tucked inside, scribbled with Sol’s handwriting and small experiment diagrams.

Sol leaned forward slightly, resting his elbows on his knees as he gestured toward it. “I’ve been researching spells here and there,” he explained, “but I haven’t found anything permanent enough for what I need.”

Bookie rustled happily at being the center of attention, his pages fluttering as Sol scratched his spine.

Sol tilted his head thoughtfully. “I’ve been considering turning him into a ginger-haired tressym,” he added. He looked down at Bookie with a fond smile. “He’d fit right in; he’s energetic, curious, and, no offense, buddy, but sometimes he lacks a few brain cells.” Sol laughed loudly as Bookie flapped his pages indignantly.

Sol snorted. “See? Perfect cat behavior.”

Auri blinked in surprise when the book drifted down the hall and dropped nearly into her lap, her hands coming up just in time to steady it. She glanced down at the title, then at the explosion of sticky notes and folded pages, and a small, impressed laugh escaped her. 

“Sol,” she said, turning the book slightly to examine the cover, “this looks less like casual research and more like the early stages of a thesis. Obviously, I approve.”

She flipped the cover open carefully, scanning a few of the colorful tabs before looking back up at him with a raised brow that carried both amusement and validation. 

“You’ve been busy.”

When he mentioned turning Bookie into a ginger-haired tressym, her attention dropped to the enchanted book, who was currently fluttering in clear protest. Auri tilted her head thoughtfully. 

“Well,” she said slowly, “a tressym disguise would solve several problems at once. People expect them to be curious, noisy, and a little chaotic.’ Her gaze flicked back up to Sol. “So yes. Bookie would blend in beautifully.” 

She gave Bookie a small, reassuring smile and a wink. “No offense intended, little one.”

Then she lifted the heavy book slightly on her lap and began flipping through the marked pages with interest.
 
“If permanence is the problem,” Auri continued, slipping easily into her thoughtful academic tone, “then we probably don’t want a single long-term glamour spell. Those tend to degrade or unravel.” 

Her finger paused at one of Sol’s diagrams.

“But,” she added, eyes lighting up slightly, “a layered illusion anchored to a physical focus might work. Something that refreshes itself automatically instead of trying to hold one perfect disguise forever. Which means your idea might be the right direction,” Auri said, tapping the page thoughtfully. “We’d just need to decide what the anchor should be.”

Auri’s words about an anchor lit a spark in Sol’s expression, an idea bursting to life in his brain.

“Oh!” He suddenly straightened, eyes wide. “Wait here, I’ll be right back!”

Before Auri could ask what he meant, Sol snapped his fingers like he was trying to hold onto the thought. Then he burst to his feet before disappearing down the hall toward his room in quick flight. Bookie rustled and sprang off the couch after him like an overly excited puppy, pages flapping wildly as he bounded down the hallway in pursuit.

Muffled sounds came from Sol’s room: drawers opening, something shifting across a desk, the faint clink of metal. A few seconds later, Sol walked back into the living room with something delicate held in his hand. Bookie hopped beside him proudly like he helped find it. Sol sat back down on the couch and opened his palm, where a golden charm bracelet rested.

The chain was delicate but well-made, and had a warm gold color that caught the lanternlight. Hanging from it was an opal crescent moon charm; the stone shifted with soft prismatic colors as it moved, flashes of green, blue, and pink swimming beneath its surface.

Sol held it out toward Auri. “What about this?” he asked. “Lucien gave it to me,” Sol explained, glancing briefly down at the bracelet before looking back at her. “He said opal is supposed to boost creativity.” His thumb brushed lightly over the charm. “So maybe it could work as the anchor?”

He gave a small shrug with hopeful eyes. “If we anchor the spell to something already tied to creative energy, maybe it might stabilize it better.”

Bookie hopped up beside him again, rustling curiously at the bracelet.

Sol laughed at Bookie. “Also,” he added with a grin, “opal is *the* gem of this fashion season.”

Auri’s eyes lit up the moment the bracelet caught the light, her attention immediately drawn to the shifting colors within the opal. She leaned in, curiosity soft and bright in her expression, her shoulder brushing lightly against Sol’s as she studied it more closely.

“That’s…a really good idea,” she said, a note of impressed warmth in her voice. Her tail gave a thoughtful sway behind her as she tilted her head, watching the way the colors moved beneath the surface. “It already has meaning, it’s tied to you— and to Lucian— and if it’s meant to enhance creativity, that actually aligns perfectly with the kind of magic we’re trying to sustain.”

“You didn’t just grab something pretty,”  she added, glancing up at him with bright eyes and a sharp grin. “You picked something *right*.”

Her fingers lifted, hesitating just a second in silent permission before she gently, if allowed, brushed the charm, careful and reverent.

“And yes,” she said, a hint of playful amusement slipping back in as her eyes flicked toward Bookie, “*Very* on trend. Bookie will be the most fashionable creature in existence.”

“I really think this could work,” she said confidently. “We can build the glamour in layers and anchor it here. The bracelet will carry the identity while the illusion refreshes itself around it.”

Her gaze lifted again, a spark of excitement threading through her calm. Careful of the opal, she took his hands in hers if allowed and squeezed, tail swaying swiftly back and forth.

“Sol…this might actually be a breakthrough…and *we* will have discovered it.”

Sol smiled as he watched Auri light up with excitement, and it widened when she called the opal *right*, and not just pretty,.

“Yeah,” he said softly. “I think so too.”

He gave her a small nod of permission as she reached for the charm, his gaze following her fingers as they brushed the opal, the colors catching and shifting under the lanternlight like a kaleidoscope. For a second, he just let himself live in the moment and relished the connection the charm gave him to his brother, and now, by extension, to Auri.

Sol squeezed Auri’s hands back, his grip warm and steady, his eyes bright. “Yeah,” he said with a certain tone. “It’ll work, I know it will!”

Sol pulled one of his hands free, rolling his shoulders slightly as his familiar spark of creative energy lit up his eyes.

“Well,” he said with a bright grin. “Let’s get to enchanting.”

Gold, silver, and bronze light sparked from his hand, as threads of magic unfurled around his fingertips like silk.
Bookie rustled excitedly, pages fluttering in applause.

Sol glanced at Auri with an ambitious glint in his eyes. “Ready when you are, *Professor*.”

Auri let out a soft breath that almost turned into a laugh at the spark in Sol’s eyes. It was familiar now, that shift in him when inspiration took hold. Her own expression brightened in answer, something focused but subtly thrilled settling over her features.

“Careful,” she teased lightly, fondness threaded in her tone as she gave him a wink. “If you keep calling me *Professor*, I might start assigning you readings.”

She shifted closer, one knee tucking beneath her as she angled toward Sol and the bracelet, her gaze sharpening as she studied the threads of magic gathering around his fingers. One hand hovered near his, just close enough to feel the flow, to understand it. 

“Alright,” she continued, voice calmer now, more focused as her academic side clicked neatly into place. “Let’s do this properly.”

Her fingers lifted slightly, and a faint, steady glow began to form at her fingertips. 

“You handle the structure,” she said, glancing at him briefly before returning her focus to the charm. “You’re better at shaping the initial weave. I’ll stabilize and reinforce to make sure it holds and doesn’t collapse in on itself. It’s a powerful spell.”

Her gaze returned to Sol, a confident smile tugging at her lips. 

“Whenever you’re ready.”
